behaviors.description: A static prompt-template lint check rejects phase-specific instruction leakage copied from SKILL.md.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure lives entirely in one function that reads template text and matches patterns — no module handoff involved. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network/subprocess; template content can be passed as a string. MOCK-HIDE: Nothing to mock. LLM-DEP: No model call. MULTI-STEP: No. EVIDENCE-LEVEL=Unit, OUTPUT-LEVEL=Unit. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no real OS needed; (b) no AI/ML; (c) no chaining. REJECTION-GATE: Integration would require a module boundary failure that does not exist here — the lint logic is a pure text-pattern check.
behaviors.test_description: Pass raw template file content (as strings) into the lint function and assert it raises or returns an error when a phrase matching SKILL.md phase-instruction vocabulary is present, and returns clean when such phrases are absent. Parameterize with known-bad and known-clean templates.

behaviors.description: The fully assembled prompt produced by the real test harness (after interpolation/fixtures) still contains no phase-instruction leakage.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure only appears after the harness wires together template loading, fixture interpolation, and prompt assembly — a single unit test on the interpolator alone would miss leakage introduced at the assembly boundary. REAL-INFRA: No network/subprocess; the harness reads fixture files from disk but an in-process run pointing at real fixture files covers this. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the interpolator hides the exact boundary where leakage is introduced. LLM-DEP: No model call needed — we inspect the assembled string, not model behavior. MULTI-STEP: No. EVIDENCE-LEVEL=Integration, OUTPUT-LEVEL=Integration. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no real network/subprocess; (b) no AI/ML output; (c) no chaining. REJECTION-GATE: System would require real OS behavior the failure does not depend on.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the real fixture loader, template interpolator, and prompt assembler together in-process with actual fixture files. Call the full assembly pipeline and assert the resulting prompt string contains no lines matching SKILL.md phase-instruction patterns. Use both a clean-template fixture (should pass) and a leaked-template fixture (should fail lint).

behaviors.description: The test invocation loads skill context from the plugin/skill files and validates behavior from that context rather than from hard-coded prompt rules.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: File loading and prompt assembly can be verified at Integration, but 'validates behavior from that context' means the test must assert that the model actually exhibits skill-driven behavior — a boundary the model introduces. REAL-INFRA: No subprocess/network beyond the LLM API. LLM-DEP: The failure mode is that behavior is driven by hard-coded instructions rather than skill context; a stub LLM would always return the same canned answer and would never reveal whether behavior is skill-driven or prompt-echo-driven. MULTI-STEP: No. EVIDENCE-LEVEL=Agentic, OUTPUT-LEVEL=Agentic. REJECTION-GATE: Integration would require the correctness check to be reducible to a deterministic assertion over a stub response; 'validates behavior from that context' is explicitly a quality judgment that depends on what a real model produces.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke the behavioral smoke test using the real LLM API, loading skill context exclusively from plugin/skill files with a user-style prompt containing no explicit phase instructions. Assert the model response passes the skill-behavior rubric AND that no hard-coded instruction appears in the assembled prompt sent to the model. Capture the sent prompt as a test artifact to confirm it contains only skill-derived context.

behaviors.description: If the relevant SKILL.md section is removed or degraded, the behavioral test fails with the same user-style prompt.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure requires the real model to respond differently when skill context is absent — no module boundary catches this. REAL-INFRA: No subprocess/network beyond the LLM API. LLM-DEP: This is the canonical LLM-DEP case: a stub always returns the same fixed response regardless of what skill context is in the prompt, so it would never fail when SKILL.md degrades. The entire point is that the real model's output changes when the guiding context is removed. MULTI-STEP: No. EVIDENCE-LEVEL=Agentic, OUTPUT-LEVEL=Agentic. REJECTION-GATE: Integration cannot disqualify because no in-process module wiring produces different deterministic output based on skill context presence.
behaviors.test_description: Parameterize the behavioral smoke test over two skill contexts: (A) full SKILL.md present, (B) relevant section removed or replaced with a degraded stub. Run both against the same user-style prompt using the real LLM API. Assert case A passes all behavioral assertions and case B causes at least one assertion to fail. This verifies the test is sensitive to skill regression, not prompt echoing.

behaviors.description: Raw model output is captured to a diagnostic artifact before any JSON parsing or extraction logic runs.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure appears at the boundary between the LLM response receiver and the parser — a unit test on either in isolation would not catch a reordering. REAL-INFRA: Writing to a diagnostic artifact involves real filesystem writes, but no subprocess/network is needed; an integration test using a real temp directory and a stub LLM response is sufficient because the failure mode is about pipeline ordering, not real-LLM content. MOCK-HIDE: A stub LLM returning a fixed string is fine here — the correctness question is whether the raw string reaches disk before parsing, not what the model said. LLM-DEP: No — the stub response reproduces the failure path exactly. MULTI-STEP: No. EVIDENCE-LEVEL=Integration, OUTPUT-LEVEL=Integration. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) filesystem writes are needed but in-process with real temp dir suffices, not true OS-level subprocess behavior; (b) no AI/ML quality judgment; (c) no chaining.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the test harness with a stub LLM client that returns a fixed raw string. Run the pipeline and assert: (1) a diagnostic artifact file exists in the configured output directory, (2) the file contains exactly the stub's raw string verbatim, and (3) if a parse error is injected at the extraction step, the artifact file is still present and complete. Use a real temp directory to verify actual filesystem writes.

behaviors.description: Assertions verify structured behavior (for example field placement or schema shape), not mere keyword presence that could come from prompt echoing.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure lives in the assertion expression itself — no module interaction is needed to detect that `'keyword' in response` was written instead of `response['field'] == value`. REAL-INFRA: None — this is a static or AST-based code check. MOCK-HIDE: Nothing to mock. LLM-DEP: No model call required. MULTI-STEP: No. EVIDENCE-LEVEL=Unit, OUTPUT-LEVEL=Unit. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no OS; (b) no AI/ML; (c) no chaining. REJECTION-GATE: Integration would require a module boundary failure that does not exist — checking assertion expression patterns is a pure syntactic or structural analysis of test source code.
behaviors.test_description: Parse the AST of all behavioral test files and assert no assertion node uses substring containment operators (`in`, `str.find`, `re.search` with a plain keyword) applied directly to a raw model output string. Assert that assertions instead use structural checks: dict-key access, Pydantic field access, schema validation calls, or equality on a parsed value. Parameterize with a known-bad assertion (keyword check) that should be rejected and a known-good assertion (field check) that should pass.
